items.correct: No — its photon energy is below the threshold required to ionise atoms; ionising radiation includes UV (high energy), X-rays and gamma.
items.misconception: Yes — all electromagnetic radiation is ionising because it carries energy.
items.random_wrong: Yes — only infrared and radio waves are non-ionising.
items.why: Students don't link photon energy (E = hf) to the ability to ionise, so they treat all EM radiation as equivalent in damage potential.

items.q: What is required to induce an emf in a coil using a magnet?
items.correct: A changing magnetic flux linkage through the coil — the magnet (or coil) must be moving or the field must be changing.
items.misconception: Just having a magnet near the coil induces an emf, even when both are stationary.
items.random_wrong: A current in the coil is what induces an emf in the magnet.
items.why: Students remember 'magnet + coil = electricity' but miss that the field must be changing for an emf to be induced (Faraday's law).

items.q: What does voltage (potential difference) across a component represent?
items.correct: The energy transferred per unit charge passing through that component.
items.misconception: Voltage is the 'amount of electricity' flowing through the wire.
items.random_wrong: Voltage measures how many electrons are stored in a battery.
items.why: Students conflate voltage with current because both are 'something to do with electricity'; they don't distinguish 'energy per charge' from 'rate of charge flow'.

items.q: Faraday's law of induction was published in:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 1831. “1801” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: If transformer has Np = 1000, Ns = 100 and Vp = 240 V AC, Vs equals:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 24 V. “240 V” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
The real figure is 1831 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
